summary: Gruber responds to misconceptions about his criticism of the Mac OS X Finder, clarifying that he doesn't think the OS 9 Finder was perfect, only that it was good enough to build upon rather than discard. He argues that the OS X Finder's browser metaphor solves a problem most Mac users didn't have, and that any upgrade to a widely-used application must prioritize keeping existing users happy. He contends that the Finder is far more than a file manager for most Mac users — it essentially is the Mac OS to them — and that the radical redesign has left the majority of long-time users feeling alienated. He concludes that simply redesigning the Finder to appeal to classic Mac users would make the entire OS X experience feel familiar to millions.
key_points: The OS X Finder's browser metaphor was a solution to a problem most Mac users didn't have — the 'too many windows' complaint was not widely shared.
When upgrading a widely-used application, keeping existing users happy must be goal number one, regardless of how well-intentioned the changes are.
For most Mac users, the Finder isn't just a file manager — it is the interface to the computer as a whole, making radical changes to it especially disorienting.
Defenders of the OS X Finder tend to be power users with Windows/Unix experience who underestimate how central the Finder is to traditional Mac users.
Most complaints about Mac OS X's interface are really complaints about the Finder specifically, and fixing it alone would transform the entire OS X experience.
money_quotes: Moving to Mac OS X is a great opportunity for developers to implement new features and new ideas, but it's not an excuse to pull the rug out from under existing users.
The browser metaphor underlying the OS X Finder is a solution to a problem that most Mac users didn't have.
When you're designing an upgrade to a widely-used application, goal number one must be to keep your existing users happy.
An upgrade that doesn't appeal to existing users is never a good idea, no matter how well intentioned the changes.
The beauty of the Macintosh is that you don't need to understand how a computer works in order to maintain control over your computer; you only need to understand how the Mac works.
If you're not a computer nerd, it is neither easy nor fun to learn to use the new Finder. It is discouraging. There is a sense of 'Jeez, what the hell is going on here?'
There is a strong sentiment among many long-time Mac users that Mac OS X is not for them. The excitement is all about how it's the best Unix ever.
key_insight: The OS X Finder's radical departure from spatial orientation was a betrayal of the users who needed it most — the non-technical majority for whom the Finder was not merely a file manager but the entire interface to their computer.
